report_name: Seven Unpatched Vulnerabilities in Widely Used FatFs Filesystem Library Expose Millions of Devices

attack_patterns.name: Exploitation for Client Execution
attack_patterns.description: An attacker with physical access can use a maliciously crafted SD card or USB drive to exploit memory corruption vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-6682, CVE-2026-6687, CVE-2026-6688) in the FatFs library to achieve arbitrary code execution on an affected device.

attack_patterns.name: Endpoint Denial of Service
attack_patterns.description: Vulnerabilities in the FatFs library, such as a divide-by-zero error (CVE-2026-6683), can be triggered by a malicious filesystem to cause the target device to crash or become permanently unresponsive (bricked).

attack_patterns.name: Supply Chain Compromise
attack_patterns.description: The widespread use of the vulnerable FatFs library in millions of embedded devices and SDKs from major vendors creates a significant supply chain risk, as downstream manufacturers must independently identify and patch the component in their products.

attack_patterns.name: Replication Through Removable Media
attack_patterns.description: The attack vector for the FatFs vulnerabilities involves an attacker with physical access introducing a maliciously crafted FAT or exFAT filesystem via removable media, such as an SD card or USB drive.

tools.name: FatFs
tools.description: A popular open-source filesystem library for embedded systems containing seven disclosed vulnerabilities that can lead to memory corruption, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6682
vulnerabilities.description: An integer overflow in the FatFs FAT32 volume mounting code can lead to a heap-based buffer overflow and potential remote code execution when parsing a malicious filesystem.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6687
vulnerabilities.description: A stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the FatFs library when handling exFAT volume labels, which can be exploited for memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6688
vulnerabilities.description: A stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the FatFs library when processing long filenames in exFAT, leading to potential memory corruption and code execution.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6685
vulnerabilities.description: A mathematical error in the FatFs library can result in silent data corruption when writing to files on a manipulated filesystem.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6683
vulnerabilities.description: A divide-by-zero error in the FatFs library can be triggered by a malicious filesystem, causing the affected device to crash or become permanently unresponsive.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6686
vulnerabilities.description: A flaw in the FatFs library can lead to data leakage from previously deleted files when parsing a crafted filesystem.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-6684
vulnerabilities.description: An issue in the FatFs library related to handling malformed GPT partition tables. This was the only flaw incidentally fixed in version R0.16.

identities.name: Espressif
identities.description: A vendor of IoT development platforms, such as ESP-IDF, that utilize the vulnerable FatFs library, making their products susceptible to the disclosed vulnerabilities.

identities.name: STMicroelectronics
identities.description: A major vendor of microcontrollers and development platforms, like STM32Cube, that incorporate the vulnerable FatFs library, exposing their products to a supply chain risk.

indicators.name: FatFs Vulnerability Exploit Attempt Causing System Crash
indicators.pattern:
title: FatFs Vulnerability Exploit Attempt Causing System Crash
id: 7c8d9e0f-1a2b-4c5d-8e9f-0a1b2c3d4e5f
status: experimental
description: Detects log messages indicative of a system crash, such as kernel panics or memory corruption errors, potentially related to exploiting vulnerabilities in the FatFs library when mounting a removable storage device.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/07/unpatched-flaws-disclosed-in-filesystem.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-04
tags:
  - attack.t1499
level: high
logsource:
  category: system
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    keywords:
      - 'kernel panic'
      - 'memory corruption'
      - 'divide by zero'
      - 'FAT-fs'
      - 'exfat'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects log messages indicative of a system crash, such as kernel panics or memory corruption errors, potentially related to exploiting vulnerabilities in the FatFs library when mounting a removable storage device.
